key_insight: 7T whole-brain MRSI showed that ALS has two separable metabolic signals: motor-cortex neuronal loss (lower tNAA) and broader glutamatergic dysregulation (higher Gln/Glx), with glutamine emerging as the more sensitive marker.
clinical_relevance: These metabolite patterns may help map ALS progression across motor regions and support biomarker development for future trial design, but they are not yet ready for routine clinical use.
method: High-resolution 7T 3D-CRT-FID-MRSI was combined with motor-cortex single-voxel sLASER in 5 people with rapidly progressing ALS and 7 controls.
Primary motor cortex was split into bulbar/face, hand/upper-limb, and foot/lower-limb zones to test somatotopic differences.
Bayesian hierarchical mixed-effects models and assurance simulations were used to estimate group effects, longitudinal change, and candidate trial endpoints.
evidence_signal: Baseline analysis showed a motor-cortex-selective tNAA/tCr deficit with elevated Gln/tCr and Glx/tNAA, and annualized metabolite slopes correlated strongly with ALSFRS-R decline in matched domains.
watch_item: The study is very small and focused on rapidly progressing ALS, so results need replication in larger, more diverse cohorts before they can guide clinical decisions.
paper_title: Glutamine and NAA dissociate in ALS across somatotopically defined motor regions using 7T MRSI
